commander_summary: The Regional Water Distribution Network is currently healthy by all measured parameters — pump loads nominal at 76.2% average, system pressure at 68.5 PSI with a 71% margin above the kill switch, reservoir at 92.4%, water quality within spec, and no kill switch approaching trigger. The call is INCREASE_MONITORING, driven not by measured deterioration but by two critical blind spots: vibration telemetry is dark on 50% of pumps and hydrant pressure covers only one sector, leaving Kill Switches #2 and #3 partially unmonitorable as a compound stress scenario builds. The thesis paradox: the Pump 4 teardown simultaneously validates system responsiveness (MWA identified and scheduled the repair) and threatens the redundancy claim (three-pump operations at ~101.5% load exceed the normal operating band). We do not know when the teardown will occur, what Pumps 2 and 3 look like under the hood, or what peak summer demand will actually require — these three unknowns define the boundary of our confidence.

recommendation_reasoning: The system is physically healthy by all current measurements, which argues against REDUCE_EXPOSURE or EXIT_SIGNAL. The approaching compound stress scenario (Pump 4 teardown + summer demand) makes closing these gaps time-sensitive. HOLD_POSITION would be appropriate only if monitoring coverage were adequate; it is not. INCREASE_MONITORING is driven by the data gaps, not by measured deterioration.

The call is INCREASE_MONITORING, driven not by measured deterioration but by two critical blind spots: vibration telemetry is dark on 50% of pumps and hydrant pressure covers only one sector, leaving Kill Switches #2 and #3 partially unmonitorable as a compound stress scenario builds. The thesis paradox: the Pump 4 teardown simultaneously validates system responsiveness (MWA identified and scheduled the repair) and threatens the redundancy claim (three-pump operations at ~101.5% load exceed the normal operating band). We do not know when the teardown will occur, what Pumps 2 and 3 look like under the hood, or what peak summer demand will actually require — these three unknowns define the boundary of our confidence.

player_analysis.stated_position: System operating optimally. Regional reservoir levels remain strong going into the summer.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Maintenance log documents Pump 4 seal weepage and schedules routine teardown. Pump 4 vibration reading of 18.5 Hz is elevated relative to Pump 1 baseline of 12.1 Hz. Pump 4 carries highest load at 78.5%. No vibration telemetry is available for Pumps 2 and 3 — either sensors are not installed, offline, or data is not being transmitted. No public timeline for the teardown has been provided.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: The public statement ('operating optimally') directly contradicts the maintenance log evidence of seal weepage and scheduled teardown. Per LZ-EH-003, the maintenance log (action-derived) carries more weight. The gap is moderate: 'optimally' is a stretch when one pump has documented degradation requiring teardown. However, the system IS currently delivering water within all measured parameters, so 'operating' is accurate — 'optimally' is the misleading modifier. The more significant behavioral signal is the absence of a public teardown timeline. An entity focused on public reassurance would rationally omit a maintenance timeline to avoid public concern, especially pre-summer.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: MWA is managing public perception while internally acknowledging mechanical degradation. This is consistent with their documented behavioral pattern of prioritizing continuity of service and public reassurance, and historically delaying capital-intensive repairs. The most rational reading of their behavior: they know Pump 4 needs work, they have logged it internally (responsible), they have not communicated urgency externally (reassurance-oriented), and they have not committed to a timeline (consistent with delay tendency). This is standard institutional behavior for a public utility — not evidence of deception, but evidence of predictable incentive-following.

The structural limitation — monitoring macro compliance without real-time telemetry — means the regulator would currently see green across all their metrics: reservoir high, system pressure well above 40 PSI, water quality within EPA standards. They have no visibility into pump-level vibration, load distribution, or the maintenance log.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: The regulator is operating within its designed (and limited) scope. There is no evidence of negligence or deliberate blindness — this is an architecture limitation. The regulator would have no rational basis to act given the data visible to them. Their behavior is consistent with their structural constraints, not with any strategic choice.

The absence could mean: (a) the system is fine and fire dispatch has no concerns, (b) fire dispatch has concerns but we lack the data channel, or (c) fire dispatch is operating normally and hasn't encountered pressure issues. feedback_loops.loop: Pump 4 Degradation → Teardown → Three-Pump Load Redistribution → Elevated Load on Remaining Pumps → Accelerated Wear → Potential Cascading Pump Degradation
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: The loop activates when Pump 4 is actually taken offline for teardown. At that point, the arithmetic shows remaining pumps would carry ~101.5% average load (above 85% normal ceiling, below 115% kill switch). The trigger from dormant to active_orderly is the teardown commencement itself. The trigger from active_orderly to active_accelerating would be concurrent summer demand spike pushing loads toward 115% or another pump developing issues. Currently dormant because all four pumps are online and operating within normal bands.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop directly challenges the thesis claim of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy during localized equipment maintenance.' The arithmetic shows that current demand levels, redistributed to three pumps, would exceed the normal operating band. Whether this constitutes a thesis challenge depends on how long the system can sustain loads in the 85-115% range — data we do not have.

feedback_loops.loop: MWA Delay Tendency → Teardown Deferred Into Summer → Higher Demand During Three-Pump Operations → Greater Stress on System → Greater Risk of Kill Switch Approach
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop is predictive and depends on MWA actually delaying the teardown. If they perform the teardown now (pre-summer, lower demand), the stress during three-pump operations is minimized. If they delay into peak summer, the compound effect is maximized. The trigger from dormant to active_orderly is evidence that the teardown is being deferred (e.g., continued Pump 4 operation with worsening vibration readings through spring). We currently have no evidence of delay beyond the entity profile's general pattern — per LZ-RC-002, a general pattern is not confirmation of specific behavior.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: If MWA delays teardown into summer peak, the thesis faces its maximum stress test. If they execute pre-summer, the system likely manages within acceptable parameters (lower demand partially offsets the loss of one pump). The timing decision is the single most consequential variable we cannot currently observe.

feedback_loops.loop: Monitoring Blind Spots → Undetected Degradation on Pumps 2/3 → Surprise Failure During Three-Pump Operations → Catastrophic Two-Pump Scenario
components: Missing vibration telemetry for Pumps 2 and 3
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop cannot be directly observed because the monitoring gap IS the problem. If Pumps 2 or 3 have vibration issues comparable to or worse than Pump 4, and one fails during Pump 4 teardown, the system drops to two pumps — a scenario far beyond anything modeled. The trigger is an unobserved pump failure, which by definition we would not see coming with current data. The loop transitions directly from dormant to critical without passing through intermediate states.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the highest-impact, lowest-probability tail scenario. It represents a monitoring architecture failure, not a measured stress. The thesis assumes redundancy, but redundancy cannot be verified when 50% of health telemetry is dark.

behavioral_patterns.pattern: Institutional reassurance coupled with incomplete disclosure — MWA publicly claims optimal operations while internally documenting degradation and scheduling maintenance. This is a common pattern in public utility operators with dual accountability (public trust + service continuity).

Regulatory architecture gap — the regulator monitors macro metrics while the operator controls micro-level data. This creates an information asymmetry where the operator can present a compliant face while managing sub-surface issues. Neither player is acting irrationally; the system architecture incentivizes this pattern.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: MWA Public Statement Minimizes Known Issues
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: MWA's public statement is standard institutional communication — all utilities describe their systems positively. 'Operating optimally' is boilerplate PR language, not a deliberate cover-up. The maintenance log is routine internal documentation. There is no strategic concealment; this is simply how utilities communicate.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA is following standard public utility communication incentives: reassure customers, avoid pre-summer anxiety, maintain public confidence. The maintenance log represents responsible internal tracking. The gap between public and internal communication is normal institutional behavior, not evidence of strategic deception.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: MWA is deliberately suppressing information about system vulnerability to avoid regulatory scrutiny or public pressure to accelerate costly repairs.
evidence_citations: Maintenance log documenting Pump 4 seal weepage and scheduled teardown
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The say-do gap is real and documented per LZ-EH-003, but the simplest explanation is standard institutional communication behavior. The maintenance log shows MWA IS tracking the issue internally and has scheduled a teardown — this is responsible behavior. The public statement omitting the issue is normal PR, not strategic concealment. The alternative (deliberate suppression) requires assuming malicious intent without evidence. Per LZ-RC-004, the simpler explanation must be tested first, and it is sufficient here.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Pump 4 Elevated Vibration + Seal Weepage + Highest Load
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Pump 4 is simply an older or more heavily used pump showing normal wear patterns. The vibration at 18.5 Hz is elevated but well within operational tolerance (53% of kill switch). Seal weepage is a common maintenance item for centrifugal pumps. The load differential of 3.7 ppt across the pump array is negligible. This is routine equipment aging, not a strategic signal.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Pump 4 is experiencing normal age-related degradation. The combination of slightly higher vibration, minor seal weepage, and marginally highest load is consistent with a pump nearing its maintenance interval. The scheduled teardown confirms the operator has recognized this and is responding appropriately within their operational framework.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Pump 4 is on an accelerating degradation curve that will force emergency teardown at an inopportune time, compounding seasonal stress.
evidence_citations: Pump 4 vibration at 18.5 Hz vs Pump 1 baseline at 12.1 Hz
Maintenance log: seal weepage, scheduled teardown
Pump 4 load at 78.5% within 70-85% normal band
strategic_inferences.reasoning: All measured values are within normal operational bands. Per LZ-RC-002, a single vibration reading cannot establish a trend. The alternative explanation (accelerating degradation) requires assuming a trend trajectory from one data point, which is structurally invalid. The null hypothesis — routine wear leading to routine maintenance — fully explains the observed data. The combination of signals is worth monitoring but does not yet require a strategic explanation.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Missing Vibration Telemetry for Pumps 2 and 3
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Sensors are not installed on Pumps 2 and 3, OR sensors are installed but not connected to the telemetry feed being analyzed, OR sensors are offline for routine maintenance/calibration. This is a monitoring infrastructure limitation, not evidence of concealment or system failure.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: This is most likely a monitoring infrastructure limitation. Many water utility pump stations have inconsistent sensor deployment — especially if vibration monitoring was added incrementally rather than as part of original installation. The absence of data reflects equipment/infrastructure limitations, not a strategic decision to hide information.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: MWA has deliberately limited telemetry to present a selectively positive picture of system health.
evidence_citations: Vibration data available for Pump 1 (12.1 Hz) and Pump 4 (18.5 Hz)
No vibration data transmitted for Pumps 2 or 3
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The alternative (deliberate concealment) requires assuming malicious intent without any supporting evidence. Per LZ-RC-004, the simpler explanation (inconsistent sensor deployment or data transmission configuration) is sufficient. The gap is real and consequential for monitoring capability, but the cause is most likely infrastructure, not strategy. The finding stands as a monitoring limitation regardless of cause.

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The thesis claim of redundancy during 'localized equipment maintenance' may simply have not been rigorously tested against current demand levels. The 101.5% calculation is arithmetic, not adversarial. The system may have operated on three pumps many times before with acceptable results — we just don't have that historical data. The gap between 85% normal ceiling and 115% kill switch may be a well-understood operational zone for the operators even if it's not documented in our data.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA operators almost certainly have institutional knowledge about three-pump operations. The thesis language ('sufficient mechanical redundancy during localized equipment maintenance') may reflect operational experience we cannot observe. The fact that they have scheduled a teardown without apparent urgency suggests they have managed this transition before.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The thesis redundancy claim has never been stress-tested and represents an optimistic assumption by management. The 101.5% load scenario reveals a genuine structural vulnerability.
assumptions: MWA has successfully operated on three pumps in the past
evidence_citations: Layer 2 arithmetic: ~76.2% avg × 4 pumps redistributed to 3 = ~101.5% per pump
Maintenance log confirms teardown is 'routine' — language suggesting this is a known, managed event
No kill switch is currently close to triggering
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: The teardown begins and the system either (a) maintains pressure and load distribution within acceptable ranges on three pumps (thesis confirmed), or (b) shows immediate load or pressure stress (thesis challenged). Observable via next system telemetry snapshot during three-pump operations.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The arithmetic challenge is real: 101.5% average load exceeds the normal band. However, 'routine teardown' language in the maintenance log suggests MWA treats this as a managed event, implying institutional experience with three-pump operations. One assumption (MWA has done this before) is required, making this a VALID inference. The null hypothesis is slightly favored because the operator's calm posture toward teardown is itself a behavioral signal per LZ-EH-003 — scheduling a 'routine' teardown implies confidence in managing the transition.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Summer Demand Approaching + Teardown Timeline Unspecified
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Summer is approaching as it does every year. The teardown is scheduled but the timeline simply hasn't been communicated publicly. MWA may have an internal schedule that targets pre-summer completion. The convergence of these two events is a calendar reality, not a strategic signal.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The timing convergence is a structural risk factor but not evidence of strategic behavior. Operators schedule maintenance when they can, and summer approaching is a predictable, non-strategic event. The unspecified timeline is consistent with internal scheduling processes that don't require public disclosure.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: MWA is delaying the teardown to avoid short-term service disruption during spring, which will push the maintenance into the higher-risk summer window.
evidence_citations: Maintenance log: 'scheduled for routine teardown' with no date specified
MWA public statement references summer reservoir levels
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The alternative (MWA deliberately delaying into summer) requires assuming (1) the teardown hasn't already been scheduled for near-term execution and (2) MWA is choosing to delay rather than simply processing internally. Per the entity profile, MWA 'historically' delays capital-intensive repairs, but per LZ-RC-002, a historical pattern does not confirm current specific behavior. The null hypothesis — normal scheduling process with no public timeline — is sufficient to explain the data.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Pump 4 Maintenance Timeline Unspecified — Potential Delay into Summer
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The teardown timeline is managed internally and not publicly disclosed. This is standard operational practice for utility maintenance. The lack of a public date does not mean delay.

Entity profile: 'Historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs'
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If Pump 4 vibration readings increase in subsequent measurements without teardown occurring, this would confirm delay. If teardown commences before summer peak, delay hypothesis is falsified.

This is one assumption — that the historical pattern applies to this specific case — making it VALID with caution. However, per LZ-EH-003, the scheduling of the teardown IS an action, and scheduling is the opposite of ignoring. The delay hypothesis is plausible but not yet supported by current evidence. The null is slightly favored.

hidden_moves.likely_action: MWA may be internally evaluating whether to redistribute Pump 4 load to other pumps ahead of teardown to extend Pump 4's operational life until post-summer, OR they may be sourcing parts/contractors for the teardown and the timeline depends on procurement — not on a deliberate decision to delay.

This could indicate they plan near-term teardown (no point in de-loading) or that they haven't considered load redistribution.

hidden_moves.likely_action: No hidden moves likely. The regulator has no signal that would trigger action. All metrics visible to them (reservoir level, system pressure, water quality) are green. There is no rational basis for hidden regulatory activity.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: All current measurements are within normal ranges: pump loads at 76.2% average (within 70-85% band), system pressure at 68.5 PSI (71% above 40 PSI kill switch), reservoir at 92.4%, water quality at 0.8 NTU, no Divergent Inventory Paradox, no kill switch close to triggering. The system is currently performing as the thesis describes. The 'routine' characterization of the Pump 4 teardown suggests institutional confidence in managing the transition.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Thesis is confirmed if the system successfully manages the Pump 4 teardown (whenever it occurs) while maintaining pressure above 40 PSI, hydrant flows above 20 PSI, and loads below 115%. Observable within weeks to months depending on teardown timing.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis claim of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy during localized equipment maintenance' may need qualification. The three-pump contingency arithmetic (101.5% average load) suggests that redundancy exists only with margin compression — the system can likely function on three pumps but NOT with the comfortable margins the thesis implies. Modification: 'The RWDN maintains operational capability during single-pump maintenance but with significantly reduced margins, particularly during peak demand periods. Redundancy is functional but not comfortable.' Additionally, the thesis claim that 'high reservoir volume guarantees long-term hydraulic stability' is too strong — reservoir level guarantees supply availability but not distribution capability, which depends on pump health.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: Three-pump load redistribution arithmetic (~101.5% per pump exceeds normal 85% ceiling); monitoring gaps on 50% of pump vibration telemetry and most sector hydrant pressures; documented MWA credibility gap between public statements and maintenance records.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: No kill switch is currently close to triggering. All measured values have significant margins. Falsification would require multiple compounding failures: Pump 4 degradation accelerating pre-teardown + teardown delayed into peak summer + unexpected failure of a second pump + demand exceeding three-pump capacity. This is a tail scenario requiring multiple independent assumptions to materialize (per LZ-EPH-003, this would be speculative).
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: System pressure dropping below 55 PSI (approaching meaningful proximity to 40 PSI kill switch), OR any pump vibration exceeding 25 Hz (concern threshold), OR evidence of Pump 4 condition worsening significantly before teardown (vibration >25 Hz, seal weepage escalating to active leak), OR discovery that Pumps 2 or 3 have hidden degradation comparable to Pump 4.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly. All stress indicators are within normal bands. No cascade mechanism is active. The stress is building in the sense that three factors are converging on a future timeline (Pump 4 degradation, unspecified teardown schedule, approaching summer demand), but none has reached a level that creates self-reinforcing feedback. The system has significant buffers: 71% pressure margin above kill switch, loads 38+ percentage points below kill switch, full reservoir. Disorderly stress would require the simultaneous failure of multiple buffers.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The most proximate catalyst is the Pump 4 teardown itself — specifically, the transition from four-pump to three-pump operations during a period of elevated demand. This is not imminent (current conditions are pre-summer, teardown date unknown) but is the foreseeable event most likely to compress margins. A second, lower-probability catalyst would be unexpected failure of another pump before or during Pump 4 teardown. A third would be an extreme demand event (heat wave, major fire) coinciding with three-pump operations. Each successive catalyst requires additional assumptions.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: Leg 1 (Pump health degradation): Mildly pre-loaded. Pump 4 shows early degradation evidence but all values within normal bands. Not critical. Leg 2 (Seasonal demand increase): Approaching but not yet arrived. Temperature at 72°F indicates pre-summer. Actual demand increase has not yet materialized in the data. Leg 3 (Maintenance timing uncertainty): Pre-loaded in the sense that the teardown is scheduled but undated. The uncertainty itself is the pre-load — the system will transition to a more vulnerable state at an unknown time. Overall: one leg showing early elevation (Pump 4 health), one approaching (summer), one loaded with timing uncertainty (teardown). No leg is at critical level. The system is NOT pre-loaded to a degree where a single shock triggers cascading failure.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis faces a paradox: the most reliable way to confirm system redundancy is to observe successful three-pump operations under stress — but that test is also the highest-risk scenario for thesis falsification. MWA must take Pump 4 offline to repair it (protecting long-term reliability), but doing so temporarily reduces redundancy (creating short-term vulnerability). Delaying the teardown preserves current four-pump operations but risks Pump 4's condition worsening to the point of unplanned failure — a worse outcome than planned teardown. The optimal play for MWA is to execute the teardown as soon as possible during lowest demand (spring), but their documented delay tendency works against this optimal timing. The thesis is simultaneously most validated and most vulnerable during the teardown period.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals a system in routine operational mode with no strategic signals: MWA is communicating optimistically while managing known degradation internally (standard institutional behavior, not deception); the regulator has no visibility into sub-surface issues and no trigger to act; fire dispatch is a dark channel providing no independent validation. The data pattern is best explained by normal equipment aging, standard institutional incentives, and monitoring infrastructure limitations — not by any strategic dynamic. The primary risk is structural and timing-dependent, not behavioral: the convergence of Pump 4 teardown, summer demand, and monitoring blind spots on 50% of pump vibration telemetry creates a foreseeable stress scenario that has not yet materialized. Layer 4 should focus on resolving the teardown timeline, acquiring Pumps 2 and 3 vibration data, and establishing the fire dispatch validation channel — these three data acquisitions would collapse most of the remaining uncertainty.

I understand the degradation trajectory concept and the thesis thresholds well enough to score.

knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: 0.8 NTU vs EPA 4 NTU limit and <1 NTU goal. System pressure well above backflow threshold. Straightforward measurement against regulatory standards.

The arithmetic is sound: ~76.2% average × 4 pumps = ~304.8 demand units; redistributed to 3 pumps = ~101.6% per pump, exceeding the 85% normal ceiling. However, this is a projection, not a measurement. Key unknowns: whether pumps can sustain >100% load temporarily, whether demand is reducible, whether load distribution would be even across remaining three pumps.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: No data on pump rated capacity vs actual capacity. 'Normal range' 70-85% may not equal maximum sustainable output. The 115% kill switch threshold suggests pumps can operate above 100% for some duration. The gap is between 'normal range ceiling' (85%) and 'kill switch' (115%) — we don't know the degradation curve in that gap.
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Severity 5. The arithmetic creates a legitimate concern but is forward-looking (LZ-RC-005). Current operations are on four pumps at nominal loads. The scenario assumes current demand levels persist during teardown, which is an assumption. However, the thesis explicitly claims redundancy during 'localized equipment maintenance' — this is a direct challenge to that claim.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Reduced by 1 due to predictive nature, but noted that this directly challenges a core thesis claim about redundancy.

compound_stress.pre_loaded_detail: The thesis context does not include a Compound Stress Matrix with USD/JPY, JGB 10Y, or Brent Crude thresholds. This is a water infrastructure thesis, not a financial instrument thesis. The compound stress framework as specified in the prompt template does not apply to this thesis domain. No macro-financial stress legs are relevant to RWDN integrity assessment. The analogous compound stress for this thesis would be: (1) Pump health degradation, (2) Seasonal demand increase, (3) Maintenance timing uncertainty — of which legs 1 and 3 show early elevation while leg 2 is approaching.
compound_stress.stress_chain_proximity: Not applicable in the financial macro sense. In thesis-relevant terms: the convergence of Pump 4 degradation + unspecified teardown timing + approaching summer demand creates a moderate compound stress scenario. No leg is at critical levels currently, but two of three are showing early signals. The system is not pre-loaded to a degree that a single shock would trigger cascading failure — current margins (pressure, reservoir, load) provide buffer.

_layer2_raw.bear_pressure_reasoning: Current measured values are all within normal ranges — no kill switch is close to triggering. Positive indicators: balanced pump loads (76.2% avg), healthy pressure (68.5 PSI, 71% above floor), high reservoir (92.4%), good water quality (0.8 NTU), no Divergent Inventory Paradox. Against this, several concerning signals converge: (1) Pump 4 shows early degradation evidence (elevated vibration + seal weepage + highest load), though all values are within normal bands; (2) Critical monitoring blind spots exist — 50% of pump vibration telemetry is dark and hydrant pressure data covers only one sector; (3) The three-pump contingency scenario (101.5% avg load per pump) directly challenges the thesis redundancy claim but is forward-looking, not current; (4) MWA credibility is degraded by the contradiction between public statement and maintenance logs; (5) Summer demand timing convergence with Pump 4 teardown is a plausible but unquantified risk. Bear pressure is moderate at 35/100. The system is currently healthy by all measured standards, but the combination of monitoring gaps, a degrading pump heading for unscheduled-timeline teardown, and approaching seasonal stress creates legitimate near-term uncertainty. No single signal is alarming; the compound effect of multiple moderate signals warrants attention. Score held below 40 because all current measurements are in normal ranges and the concerning scenarios are predictive, not measured (per LZ-RC-005).
_layer2_raw.layer2_summary: RWDN is currently healthy by all measurable standards: loads nominal, pressure strong, reservoir full, water quality within spec, no kill switches approaching trigger. Primary concern is a forward-looking compound stress scenario: Pump 4 shows early mechanical degradation (vibration 18.5 Hz, seal weepage, highest load at 78.5%) with an unspecified teardown timeline, approaching summer peak demand, and a three-pump contingency calculation showing loads would exceed normal ceiling (~101.5%). Critical monitoring gaps exist: 50% of pump vibration telemetry is dark (Pumps 2 and 3) and hydrant pressure data covers only Sector B, meaning Kill Switches #2 and #3 cannot be fully monitored. MWA public statements contradict maintenance logs, reducing their value as an information source. Layer 3 should prioritize: (1) acquiring vibration data for Pumps 2 and 3, (2) obtaining Pump 4 teardown timeline, (3) assessing three-pump operational capacity under peak demand, and (4) establishing fire dispatch as an independent validation channel. Bear pressure 35/100 — moderate, driven primarily by monitoring gaps and predictive stress convergence rather than current measured deterioration.
